articles.title: An ordinary laptop solved a problem thought to require a quantum computer
articles.summary: A quantum problem once described as impossible for classical computers has now been solved using relatively modest hardware. Researchers used tensor networks to compress the overwhelming wave function created by hundreds of entangled qubits, allowing some calculations to run on a laptop. Their results matched both theoretical predictions and simulations performed with a quantum computer. The method could open new paths for exploring quantum dynamics and materials.
articles.content:
Physicists have used an ordinary computer, advanced mathematics, and specialized software to solve a difficult quantum physics problem that had been described as beyond the reach of classical machines.

The work was carried out by researchers at the Center for Computational Quantum Physics (CCQ) at the Simons Foundation's Flatiron Institute, together with collaborators at Boston University. Their method proved efficient enough for some of the calculations to run on a personal laptop.

By extracting more computing power from conventional hardware, the approach could expand the range of quantum dynamics problems scientists can study. It may also offer a useful strategy for optimization problems in which researchers must identify the best answer among many possible solutions.

The findings were published in the journal Science.

Simulating Hundreds of Interacting Qubits

The challenge involved modeling hundreds of interacting 'qubits,' the quantum counterparts of the bits used by traditional computers. The qubits were arranged in square, cubic, or diamond shaped lattices.

A conventional bit stores either a 0 or a 1. A qubit, however, can exist in a superposition of multiple states. This feature gives quantum systems their unusual capabilities, but it also makes their behavior extremely difficult to reproduce on a classical computer.

In a March 2025 article, also published in Science, another research team reported using a quantum computer to calculate the dynamics of an especially complex qubit system. That team argued that a classical computer could not match the achievement.

"Whenever we [at the CCQ] see these kinds of claims, we're always a bit skeptical," says Joseph Tindall, an associate research scientist at the CCQ and first author on the new Science paper. "Like, 'Did you try this? Did you try that?'"

For the CCQ researchers, the claim offered a compelling way to test the limits of their own techniques.

The problem served as an opportunity to take their tools "out for a test drive," says study co-author and CCQ research scientist Miles Stoudenmire. "We could have picked some more arbitrary target," Stoudenmire says. "But it was like 'Why not pick this one that has a big claim attached to it?'"

The Challenge of Quantum Entanglement

One of the greatest obstacles was quantum entanglement. When qubits become entangled, their properties remain connected, even when the qubits are separated by large distances. As a result, researchers cannot model each qubit independently.

Instead, sophisticated algorithms are needed to describe the entire system.

"When you have lots of particles that interact by quantum physics, you have this wave function that describes the state of the system," Tindall says. "It's this huge object that rapidly gets bigger and bigger the more particles there are."

The wave function contains the information needed to describe the quantum system, but its size increases exponentially as more particles are added.

As the wave function's size grows exponentially, "I just can't directly store it on my computer," he says. Working with such enormous wave functions is a recurring problem in quantum physics. Yet these calculations are essential for predicting the behavior of quantum materials, including superconductors.

Compressing a Vast Quantum System

The researchers overcame this barrier by developing and applying new tools based on tensor networks. These mathematical structures compress the information contained in a wave function so that it can be handled more efficiently.

Tindall compares the approach to "a zip file for the wave function where you've taken all this information, and you've compressed it into this mathematical data structure full of these small tables of numbers that are interconnected to each other."

That compression made the simulation manageable on classical computers. Tindall completed many of the first calculations on a laptop using ITensor, a high-performance tensor network software library created at the CCQ.

The new simulations also demonstrate how the ITensor team is adapting tensor techniques for new types of problems. In this case, the researchers modeled three-dimensional quantum dynamics with a 3D tensor network.

"It's this very powerful compression that can be very effective, but it's a pretty complex mathematical object," Tindall says. "This really is a bit of a frontier, because working with these objects -- especially in three dimensions -- is very untrodden. You need sophisticated codes and algorithms to deal with them; it's a software engineering challenge in itself."

An Older Algorithm Finds a New Use

Many of the simulations required only relatively modest computing resources. For the early calculations, Tindall used belief propagation, an algorithm developed in the 1980s that researchers have recently adapted for quantum systems.

"It's a little more approximate than some of the other methods, but it's way cheaper, and we can run it much more directly on lots of harder problems," Stoudenmire says.

He contrasts that with "more sophisticated methods in the past of our field" that "wouldn't be able to even start going for some of these three-dimensional problems, because they're so big."

Although the hardware was modest, the results reached state-of-the-art levels of accuracy. The simulations produced solutions that aligned with theoretical predictions and performed well on smaller problems where the correct answers could be checked.

Most importantly, the results agreed with those previously obtained using a quantum computer. The difference was that the new calculations did not require quantum hardware.

Classical and Quantum Computing Can Work Together

The findings add to the debate over where classical computing ends and quantum advantage begins. However, Tindall and Stoudenmire emphasize that the two fields are not simply competing with each other.

Classical simulations can help researchers understand what quantum computers are capable of doing, while progress in quantum hardware can inspire new classical methods.

"The good side of the classical versus quantum computing debate is that there's a lot of synergy between the kind of simulations we're interested in and the codes we write and what can be realized on these quantum computers," Tindall says. "That can help guide us, and it can also help guide quantum computing researchers, because, obviously, the barrier for entry for us to simulate certain things is a lot easier than for them, because we don't have to build a quantum computer. I can just write some code and press 'run' on my personal computer."

The Next Quantum Simulation Challenge

The researchers are now developing methods that go beyond systems made only of qubits. Their next goal is to model electrons that can move between different sites.

These systems are significantly more difficult to simulate, but they are also directly relevant to understanding real quantum materials.

"They're really, quantitatively, a lot harder problems," Stoudenmire says. "So that's one of our next big bars that we want to clear."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough in quantum physics and computing, where researchers solved a complex quantum problem previously thought to require quantum computers using classical hardware like an ordinary laptop. This advancement has broad implications for quantum research, materials science, and optimization problems, demonstrating a meaningful technological innovation with potential large-scale benefits for science and society.
articles.personality_title: Researchers solve a tough quantum problem using an ordinary laptop
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Quantum computers use qubits that can be in many states at once, making them powerful but hard to simulate on normal computers. Scientists thought some quantum problems needed quantum machines to solve.

**What happened** – A team from the Simons Foundation and Boston University used special math called tensor networks to compress the huge quantum data. This allowed them to run complex quantum calculations on a regular laptop. Their results matched those from actual quantum computers and theory.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows that some quantum problems can be solved without expensive quantum hardware. It opens new ways to study quantum materials and solve difficult questions using everyday computers.

**What's next step** – The researchers want to tackle even harder problems involving moving electrons in materials. These are more complex but closer to real-world materials, which could help develop future technologies.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By using clever math and software, scientists solved a quantum problem on a laptop that was once thought to need a quantum computer.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des chercheurs résolvent un problème quantique difficile avec un simple ordinateur portable
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les ordinateurs quantiques utilisent des qubits capables d’être dans plusieurs états à la fois, ce qui les rend puissants mais difficiles à simuler sur des ordinateurs classiques. Certains problèmes quantiques semblaient nécessiter ces machines spéciales.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe du Simons Foundation et de l’université de Boston a utilisé des mathématiques spéciales appelées réseaux tensorielles pour compresser les énormes données quantiques. Cela leur a permis de faire des calculs complexes sur un simple ordinateur portable. Leurs résultats correspondent à ceux obtenus avec des ordinateurs quantiques réels et à la théorie.

**Impact** – C’est important car cela montre que certains problèmes quantiques peuvent être résolus sans matériel quantique coûteux. Cela ouvre de nouvelles voies pour étudier les matériaux quantiques et résoudre des questions difficiles avec des ordinateurs ordinaires.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent maintenant s’attaquer à des problèmes encore plus complexes impliquant des électrons mobiles dans les matériaux. Ces problèmes sont plus difficiles mais plus proches des matériaux réels, ce qui pourrait aider à développer de futures technologies.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Grâce à des mathématiques et un logiciel ingénieux, des scientifiques ont résolu un problème quantique sur un ordinateur portable, alors que l’on pensait qu’il fallait un ordinateur quantique.
articles.personality_title_es: Investigadores resuelven un problema cuántico difícil usando una laptop común
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las computadoras cuánticas usan qubits que pueden estar en muchos estados a la vez, lo que las hace poderosas pero difíciles de simular en computadoras normales. Se pensaba que algunos problemas cuánticos requerían máquinas cuánticas para resolverse.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de la Fundación Simons y la Universidad de Boston usó matemáticas especiales llamadas redes tensoriales para comprimir la enorme información cuántica. Esto les permitió hacer cálculos complejos en una laptop común. Sus resultados coincidieron con los de computadoras cuánticas reales y con la teoría.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque demuestra que algunos problemas cuánticos se pueden resolver sin hardware cuántico costoso. Abre nuevas formas de estudiar materiales cuánticos y resolver preguntas difíciles usando computadoras comunes.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren enfrentar problemas aún más difíciles que involucran electrones moviéndose en materiales. Estos son más complejos pero están más cerca de materiales reales, lo que podría ayudar a desarrollar futuras tecnologías.

**Resumen en una frase** – Usando matemáticas y software inteligentes, científicos resolvieron un problema cuántico en una laptop que antes se pensaba que solo podía resolverse con una computadora cuántica.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a vintage laptop resting on a wooden desk, connected by glowing, intertwined strands of colorful, geometric lattice structures symbolizing qubits and quantum entanglement, with intricate, softly illuminated tensor network patterns resembling compressed data flowing between them, set against a calm, abstract background evoking the frontier of quantum physics and classical computing synergy.

articles.title: Daily briefing: CRISPR gets an AI-designed upgrade
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 17 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02272-3Synthetic CRISPR proteins edit the genome more efficiently than their natural counterparts. Plus, a rocky exoplanet with an atmosphere and the mathematical wonderland of footballs.
articles.content:
Synthetic CRISPR proteins edit the genome more efficiently than their natural counterparts. Plus, a rocky exoplanet with an atmosphere and the mathematical wonderland of footballs.

Hello Nature readers, would you like to get this Briefing in your inbox free every day? Sign up here.

This dagger was buried alongside Ita, a daughter of the pharaoh Amenemhat II, who “was a young woman aged between 28 and 34 with strong upper-body muscle attachments, suggesting she habitually used weapons like maces or daggers”, says Hashesh. (Sameh Abdel Mohsen)

The remains of some ancient Egyptian princesses of the Late Middle Kingdom, who were buried with beautiful weapons, show signs that they participated in strenuous, dangerous activities such as archery and hunting. “Their bones developed to sustain heavy muscle use, which corresponds directly to the weapons discovered in their tombs,” says archeologist and study co-author Zeinab Hashesh. “These findings challenge the traditional view that elite Egyptian women led passive, sedentary lives.”

Scientific American | 7 min read

Reference: Frontiers in Environmental Archaeology paper

Astronomers have spotted a rocky exoplanet that looks likely to have an atmosphere — and could be a host for life. The planet, LHS 1140b, appears to have a helium-rich upper atmosphere and sits in the habitable zone around a star, the area in which an orbiting planet could maintain liquid water on its surface. Researchers can’t say for sure whether there is water on the exoplanet, but the findings constitute an “amazing missing piece of the puzzle” as to whether rocky exoplanets can have atmospheres, says astrophysicist Sara Seager.

Nature | 5 min read

Reference: Science paper

Scientists have used artificial intelligence to design synthetic CRISPR proteins that can edit the genome more efficiently than their natural counterparts. Many of these proteins differed markedly from the originals, with some differing by roughly 30% of their amino acid sequences. Nevertheless, the synthetic versions were still able to edit genes in bacterial, plant and human cells, a finding that’s “pretty exciting” says biochemist and study co-author Jennifer Doudna, who shared the 2020 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for her work on CRISPR systems.

Nature | 5 min read

Reference: Science paper

Both Democrat and Republican lawmakers in the United States expressed concerns about China exploiting US research at a hearing on Wednesday. But they differed on how to protect and fund US research. The hearing follows an announcement by the US National Science Foundation, which funds about a quarter of basic US academic research, that it will ban collaborations with some international institutions, including many leading Chinese universities.

Nature | 8 min read

Source: Dimensions

Features & opinion

From classic 32-panel footballs to buckyballs — the carbon molecule Buckminsterfullerene C 60 — truncated icosahedrons are lots of fun. The ball being batted around in the World Cup is a curvy version nicknamed ‘Trionda’, a spherical tetrahedron. Discover more marvellous maths — including some weird and wonderful variations brought to life.

The New York Times | 11 min read

A forgotten stack of postcards is an unwelcome temptation in the latest short story for Nature’s Futures series.

Nature | 6 min read

Andrew Robinson’s pick of the top five science books to read this week includes the science of pain and an argument against a genetic basis for wrongdoing.

Nature | 4 min read

Naked mole rat (Heterocephalus glaber) queens produce a chemical that alters the production of hormones in other females, which stifles their ability to reproduce. The smell of this chemical alone is enough to maintain the strict social structure of naked mole-rat colonies, even when the queen isn’t physically present, researchers found. Identifying the specific cue that queens use to suppress reproduction in the rest of their colony is “like the holy grail for naked mole-rat research”, behavioural neuroscientist Melissa Holmes tells the Nature Podcast.

Nature Podcast | 23 min listen

Subscribe to the Nature Podcast on Apple Podcasts, Spotify or YouTube Music, or use the RSS feed.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant scientific breakthrough where artificial intelligence has been used to design synthetic CRISPR proteins that edit genomes more efficiently than natural ones. This advancement has broad implications for genetic research and potential medical, agricultural, and biological applications, benefiting society at large. The article provides enough context about the achievement and its significance, making it a substantial and inspiring piece of good news.
articles.personality_title: AI designs new CRISPR proteins that edit genes more efficiently
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – CRISPR proteins are tools scientists use to edit genes, which means changing DNA to fix problems or improve traits. Normally, these proteins come from nature, but researchers want to make better versions that work faster and more precisely.

**What happened** – Scientists used artificial intelligence (AI) to create new, synthetic CRISPR proteins. These new proteins are quite different from natural ones, with changes in about 30% of their building blocks called amino acids. Despite these changes, the synthetic proteins were able to edit genes in bacteria, plants, and human cells even better than natural CRISPR proteins. This work was published in a science journal in July 2026.

**Impact** – This is important because gene editing can help treat diseases, improve crops, and study biology. Making CRISPR proteins that work more efficiently could speed up research and lead to better medical treatments or stronger plants. Using AI to design these proteins shows a new way to improve tools in science beyond what nature provides.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will likely test these synthetic CRISPR proteins more in different cells and organisms. They may also try to design even better versions using AI. If successful, this approach could become common for creating new gene-editing tools and help develop cures or solutions for many challenges.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have used AI to design new CRISPR proteins that edit genes more efficiently than natural ones, opening the door to faster and better genetic research and treatments.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’IA conçoit de nouvelles protéines CRISPR qui modifient les gènes plus efficacement
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les protéines CRISPR sont des outils que les scientifiques utilisent pour modifier les gènes, c’est-à-dire changer l’ADN pour corriger des problèmes ou améliorer des caractéristiques. Habituellement, ces protéines viennent de la nature, mais les chercheurs veulent créer de meilleures versions qui fonctionnent plus rapidement et avec plus de précision.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont utilisé l’intelligence artificielle (IA) pour créer de nouvelles protéines CRISPR synthétiques. Ces nouvelles protéines sont assez différentes des protéines naturelles, avec des changements dans environ 30 % de leurs éléments de base appelés acides aminés. Malgré ces changements, les protéines synthétiques ont pu modifier les gènes dans des bactéries, des plantes et des cellules humaines, et même mieux que les protéines CRISPR naturelles. Ce travail a été publié dans une revue scientifique en juillet 2026.

**Impact** – Cela est important parce que la modification des gènes peut aider à traiter des maladies, améliorer les cultures et étudier la biologie. Créer des protéines CRISPR qui fonctionnent plus efficacement pourrait accélérer la recherche et conduire à de meilleurs traitements médicaux ou à des plantes plus résistantes. Utiliser l’IA pour concevoir ces protéines montre une nouvelle façon d’améliorer les outils scientifiques au-delà de ce que la nature offre.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont probablement tester davantage ces protéines CRISPR synthétiques dans différents types de cellules et d’organismes. Ils pourraient aussi essayer de concevoir des versions encore meilleures grâce à l’IA. Si cela réussit, cette méthode pourrait devenir courante pour créer de nouveaux outils d’édition génétique et aider à développer des traitements ou solutions pour de nombreux défis.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont utilisé l’IA pour concevoir de nouvelles protéines CRISPR qui modifient les gènes plus efficacement que les protéines naturelles, ouvrant la voie à une recherche génétique et des traitements plus rapides et meilleurs.
articles.personality_title_es: La IA diseña nuevas proteínas CRISPR que editan genes con más eficiencia
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las proteínas CRISPR son herramientas que los científicos usan para editar genes, es decir, cambiar el ADN para corregir problemas o mejorar características. Normalmente, estas proteínas provienen de la naturaleza, pero los investigadores quieren crear versiones mejores que funcionen más rápido y con mayor precisión.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos usaron inteligencia artificial (IA) para crear nuevas proteínas CRISPR sintéticas. Estas nuevas proteínas son bastante diferentes de las naturales, con cambios en cerca del 30% de sus bloques de construcción llamados aminoácidos. A pesar de estos cambios, las proteínas sintéticas pudieron editar genes en bacterias, plantas y células humanas, y lo hicieron mejor que las proteínas CRISPR naturales. Este trabajo fue publicado en una revista científica en julio de 2026.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque la edición genética puede ayudar a tratar enfermedades, mejorar cultivos y estudiar la biología. Crear proteínas CRISPR que funcionen con más eficiencia podría acelerar la investigación y llevar a mejores tratamientos médicos o plantas más fuertes. Usar IA para diseñar estas proteínas muestra una nueva manera de mejorar las herramientas científicas más allá de lo que la naturaleza ofrece.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores probablemente probarán estas proteínas CRISPR sintéticas en diferentes células y organismos. También podrían intentar diseñar versiones aún mejores usando IA. Si tienen éxito, este método podría volverse común para crear nuevas herramientas de edición genética y ayudar a desarrollar curas o soluciones para muchos retos.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos usaron IA para diseñar nuevas proteínas CRISPR que editan genes con más eficiencia que las naturales, abriendo la puerta a una investigación genética y tratamientos más rápidos y mejores.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing double helix entwined with vibrant, stylized molecular shapes representing synthetic CRISPR proteins, set against a softly illuminated backdrop of abstract DNA strands and gently swirling light patterns symbolizing AI design and genome editing innovation.

articles.title: AI’s solution to 87-year-old riddle takes mathematicians by surprise
articles.summary: Mathematicians have been trying to prove the Jacobian conjecture for nearly a century, but now the Claude Fable 5 AI has apparently found a counterexample that disproves it
articles.content:
Mathematicians have been trying to prove the Jacobian conjecture for nearly a century, but now the Claude Fable 5 AI has apparently found a counterexample that disproves it

Levent Alpöge announced the solution on X levent via Twitter

A mathematician has cracked an 87-year-old conundrum with the help of AI and announced the solution unceremoniously in a tweet. The finding is the most difficult mathematical problem yet solved by AI, say experts.

Levent Alpöge at Harvard University wrote on X on 19 July that the Jacobian conjecture – which academics have spent decades trying to prove was true – is actually false, giving a tiny, 216-character counterexample as proof.

The Jacobian conjecture – which suggests that a certain type of mathematical function would also work in reverse – was formally set out by Ott-Heinrich Keller in 1939. It was also on an influential list of 18 fiendishly difficult problems for mathematicians to tackle in the 21st century drawn up by Stephen Smale in 1998.

Advertisement

Alpöge did not respond to New Scientist’s request for interview, but said in his tweet that part of the work was down to his “close friend fable”- seemingly referring to AI company Anthropic’s Claude Fable 5. Alpöge thanked Fable for working during the World Cup final.

Anthropic did not respond to a request for comment.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Daily Sign up to newsletter

Abhishek Saha at Queen Mary University of London says AI’s recent advances in mathematics, such as the OpenAI model that recently cracked a decades-old conjecture by Paul Erdős, have been surprising, but this latest finding has stepped things up significantly.

“Probably this is the biggest conjecture that AI has played a significant role [in proving or disproving] so far in mathematics,” he says. “This is a pretty big deal. AI has [made] remarkable progress in the last year.”

The single line of mathematics posted by Alpöge was simple to verify and many mathematicians have already done so, says Saha. Now the big question is how it was done.

“There are some problems that are very hard to solve but once a solution is there, they are relatively easy to check. So this is like that,” he says. “I don’t know how he did it, what exactly was the prompt to give Fable, because if one were to search everything, it wouldn’t quite work, so obviously there was some insight also which is not currently published.”

Saha says both the appearance and the nature of the result is a surprise. “People have been trying to prove it [the Jacobian conjecture] because it sounds, intuitively, very true. I don’t think that many people have been trying to disprove it. And now we have this one-sentence counterexample,” he says.

There are still open questions, says Saha. For example, this new counterexample disproves the conjecture with three variables, but a version with two variables could theoretically still be true.

Chris Bowman-Scargill at the University of York, UK, says mathematicians have in some ways already adjusted to the shocking new capabilities of AI, but there is a difference between finding counterexamples that disprove conjectures and building whole new branches of mathematics, which still requires human creativity.

“If you look at Fermat’s last theorem [which was solved by Andrew Wiles in 1994], you had to create a hundred pages of new mathematics – you had to build a whole big theory in order to solve a conjecture,” says Bowman-Scargill. “And often the interesting stuff in maths isn’t ‘oh, we’ve ticked off this conjecture, yay’, it’s more the stuff you have to build along the way in order to solve the conjecture.”

“I think [AI] has sort of proven that it can do this, so you’re now like, OK, what next?,” he says.

Ivan Fesenko at Westlake University in China believes what comes next is increasingly capable AI models that will solve ever more complex problems, disrupting the field as they go.

“Right now, AI can already produce master’s degrees in mathematics. In one year, they will produce PhD degrees in mathematics. And then the question arises, do we really need so many mathematicians around if AI can do such things so nicely?” says Fesenko. “So basically we’re talking about fundamental change in mathematics.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough where AI has helped disprove an 87-year-old major mathematical conjecture, marking a remarkable advancement in AI-assisted problem solving with broad implications for mathematics and technology.
articles.personality_title: AI helps solve 87-year-old math mystery by finding a counterexample
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For nearly 90 years, mathematicians have tried to prove the Jacobian conjecture, a difficult math problem first stated in 1939. This conjecture suggested that certain math functions could be reversed perfectly, but no one had been able to prove it true or false.

**What happened** – On July 19, Levent Alpöge, a mathematician at Harvard University, announced on the social media platform X that the Jacobian conjecture is actually false. He shared a very short example, just 216 characters long, that disproves the conjecture. Alpöge credited an AI called Claude Fable 5, developed by the company Anthropic, for helping find this example.

**Impact** – This is the most difficult math problem solved with the help of AI so far. Experts say this discovery is surprising because many mathematicians believed the conjecture was true and tried to prove it rather than disprove it. The finding shows how AI can help solve complex problems that have puzzled humans for decades. It also raises new questions, like whether smaller versions of the problem might still be true.

**What's next step** – Mathematicians will study this new counterexample carefully and explore if other versions of the conjecture hold. Researchers also expect AI tools to become even better at tackling tough math questions, possibly changing how math research is done in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time, AI has helped disprove a nearly century-old math problem, opening new paths for future discoveries in mathematics.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’IA aide à résoudre une énigme mathématique vieille de 87 ans en trouvant un contre-exemple
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis près de 90 ans, les mathématiciens tentent de prouver la conjecture jacobienne, un problème mathématique difficile formulé en 1939. Cette conjecture suggérait que certaines fonctions mathématiques pouvaient être inversées parfaitement, mais personne n’avait pu prouver si c’était vrai ou faux.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 19 juillet, Levent Alpöge, mathématicien à l’Université de Harvard, a annoncé sur le réseau social X que la conjecture jacobienne est en réalité fausse. Il a partagé un exemple très court, de seulement 216 caractères, qui infirme la conjecture. Alpöge a attribué une partie de cette découverte à une intelligence artificielle appelée Claude Fable 5, développée par la société Anthropic.

**Impact** – C’est le problème mathématique le plus difficile résolu avec l’aide d’une IA jusqu’à présent. Les experts trouvent cette découverte surprenante car beaucoup de mathématiciens pensaient que la conjecture était vraie et essayaient de la prouver plutôt que de la réfuter. Cette avancée montre comment l’IA peut aider à résoudre des problèmes complexes qui ont intrigué les humains pendant des décennies. Elle soulève aussi de nouvelles questions, notamment si des versions plus simples du problème pourraient encore être vraies.

**Prochaines étapes** – Les mathématiciens vont étudier ce nouveau contre-exemple en détail et vérifier si d’autres versions de la conjecture tiennent toujours. Les chercheurs s’attendent aussi à ce que les outils d’IA deviennent encore meilleurs pour aborder des questions mathématiques difficiles, ce qui pourrait changer la façon dont la recherche en mathématiques est menée à l’avenir.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Pour la première fois, l’IA a aidé à réfuter un problème mathématique vieux de près d’un siècle, ouvrant la voie à de nouvelles découvertes en mathématiques.
articles.personality_title_es: La IA ayuda a resolver un misterio matemático de 87 años encontrando un contraejemplo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante casi 90 años, los matemáticos han intentado probar la conjetura de Jacobiana, un problema matemático difícil planteado en 1939. Esta conjetura sugería que ciertas funciones matemáticas podían invertirse perfectamente, pero nadie había logrado demostrar si era verdad o mentira.

**Qué pasó** – El 19 de julio, Levent Alpöge, un matemático de la Universidad de Harvard, anunció en la red social X que la conjetura de Jacobiana es en realidad falsa. Compartió un ejemplo muy corto, de solo 216 caracteres, que refuta la conjetura. Alpöge agradeció a una inteligencia artificial llamada Claude Fable 5, creada por la empresa Anthropic, por ayudar a encontrar este ejemplo.

**Impacto** – Este es el problema matemático más difícil resuelto con la ayuda de la IA hasta ahora. Los expertos dicen que este descubrimiento es sorprendente porque muchos matemáticos creían que la conjetura era cierta y trataban de probarla en lugar de refutarla. El hallazgo muestra cómo la IA puede ayudar a resolver problemas complejos que han desconcertado a los humanos durante décadas. También abre nuevas preguntas, como si versiones más simples del problema podrían seguir siendo ciertas.

**Qué sigue** – Los matemáticos estudiarán este nuevo contraejemplo con atención y explorarán si otras versiones de la conjetura se mantienen. También se espera que las herramientas de IA mejoren aún más para enfrentar preguntas matemáticas difíciles, lo que podría cambiar la forma en que se realiza la investigación matemática en el futuro.

**Resumen en una frase** – Por primera vez, la IA ayudó a refutar un problema matemático de casi un siglo, abriendo nuevos caminos para futuros descubrimientos en matemáticas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an ancient, intricate mathematical manuscript gently illuminated by a glowing, abstract AI entity shaped like a softly pulsing neural network, collaboratively revealing a delicate, glowing counterexample symbol—a small, precise geometric figure—hovering between them, set against a calm, neutral background in natural, muted earth tones.

articles.title: Strange paraparticles could be reality’s missing ingredient
articles.summary: Neither matter nor force, paraparticles would be something else entirely. Fresh hints that they exist are raising hopes that they could explain reality at its deepest level
articles.content:
Neither matter nor force, paraparticles would be something else entirely. Fresh hints that they exist are raising hopes that they could explain reality at its deepest level

Stephan Schmitz

Order and chaos. Nature and nurture. Yin and yang. We are fond of carving the world into two. And physics, at first, seems to oblige. Take the universe’s unruly zoo of fundamental particles and it seems to fall into two camps. Boil it all down and you have matter and forces. Together, they make up a pleasingly simple recipe for reality.

Except physics has a way of spoiling simple recipes. In recent mathematical work, researchers have discovered a potential third category of particles. These so-called paraparticles would be neither matter nor force, but something stranger in between. And unlike either bosons or fermions, they would obey an alternate set of quantum rules, first proposed decades ago, then largely dismissed.

Thanks to new mathematical insights, paraparticles may be more viable than anyone thought. No one has seen one in nature, but the stakes are high. If paraparticles exist, it would shake our understanding of reality.

Advertisement

The standard model of particle physics, our best current theory of the fundamental ingredients of the universe, already has gaps: it omits gravity and can’t explain dark matter or dark energy. But paraparticles might offer a new way into those missing parts. “This would be huge,” says Vlatko Vedral at the University of Oxford. “It could mean the standard model is badly wrong.”

The binary at the heart of reality

To understand why paraparticles once seemed impossible, it helps to return to the two kinds of particle we know exist. There are fermions, such as electrons and quarks, which make up matter. And there are bosons, such as photons and gluons, which carry forces. Together, they compose all the fundamental particles in the standard model. Matter and messenger: the division between the two isn’t merely superficial; it seems to be written into the very mathematical structure of quantum mechanics.

Quantum mechanics treats particles in a strange but strict way. Two electrons aren’t just very alike. As far as the theory is concerned, they are interchangeable. Swap them in the maths and no experiment should be able to tell that anything has happened.

The maths in question is the wave function: a mathematical object that encodes the probabilities of finding them in particular states, defined by properties such as location, energy and spin – a kind of internal angular momentum. Wave functions contain odd-looking components, including negative signs and imaginary numbers. But experiments only ever yield ordinary probabilities. To get these, physicists square the wave function, making some of the mathematical oddness disappear from view.

Fermions such as neutrons can’t share the same quantum state. In a neutron star, this creates a quantum pressure that helps stop gravity crushing the star further ESA

All this means that, when two identical particles are swapped, there are two possible outcomes: the wave function can stay as it is or it can flip sign. After squaring, both options give the same positive probabilities. But physically, they describe very different kinds of particles.

In one case, identical particles can crowd together. These are bosons, a family that includes photons, which mediate the electromagnetic force, and gluons, which carry the strong nuclear force that binds quarks into protons and neutrons. Because bosons can have the same quantum properties at the same time, they tend to be quite gregarious. Photons, for instance, can pile together in the beam of a laser.

In the other case, identical particles are barred from sharing a quantum state. These are fermions, including electrons, quarks and ghostly particles known as neutrinos. They are mathematically forbidden from overlapping, making electrons antisocial and explaining why they occupy different shells around atomic nuclei. It is also what gives ordinary matter its stubborn solidity: squeeze atoms too tightly and their electrons have nowhere new to go, producing a quantum pressure that resists further compression.

For decades, that seemed to exhaust all the possibilities. There was no obvious third option. But in 1953, physicist Herbert Green found one. He suggested that the wave function might have more structure inside it than physicists had assumed. When two identical particles were swapped, the wave function needn’t simply stay the same or flip sign. It could also rearrange hidden internal states that don’t show up directly in ordinary measurements.

Think of two identical-looking boxes. From the outside, swapping them changes nothing: the same boxes are still there, and any ordinary inspection gives the same result. But what if there are unseen compartments inside? If so, the external sameness is preserved, even though something concealed within has changed. These are often referred to as “hidden states”, and the particles that possessed them were named “paraparticles”.

These hidden states became a source of scepticism around paraparticles. No one knew what they corresponded to physically. They might be nothing more than a mathematical trick. Or they might turn out to be similar to spin – a quantum mechanical property with no straightforward classical equivalent – except hidden from ordinary measurement. “The physical meaning attached to them is going to be to be dependent on what they actually are in a real physical system,” says Kaden Hazzard at Rice University in Texas.

Photons are bosons, particles that can pile into the same quantum state – the trick that makes technologies like lasers possible UKraft / Alamy Stock Photo

Because of this, Green faced an uphill struggle. “Already at the beginning, people started asking: ‘If these exist, why don’t we observe them?’” says Francesco Toppan, a theoretical physicist at the Brazilian Center for Research in Physics in Rio de Janeiro. “People argued you can’t see them because they are actually equivalent to bosons and fermions.”

In 1971, that argument seemed to triumph. Three physicists – Sergio Doplicher, Rudolf Haag and John E. Roberts, known as DHR – showed that, in a three-dimensional world, any theory of paraparticles could be recast as ordinary standard-model physics in disguise. Space itself would force paraparticles to behave as either fermions or bosons, and their verdict soon hardened into textbook wisdom: paraparticles were a fun idea, but not real.

Still, some were undeterred. In the 1980s, Nobel laureate Frank Wilczek showed that particles confined to two dimensions – as they can be within thin layers of materials – could indeed obey different rules. He called them “anyons” and they were a remarkable challenge to the accepted orthodoxy. “When I first proposed anyons, I thought of it as just kind of a lark and a curiosity,” says Wilczek. “But soon they turned up experimentally.”

Not as free-floating fundamental particles, however. Anyons emerge in certain materials and are known as “quasiparticles” – collective disturbances that behave like particles. A phonon is a simpler example. It is essentially a packet of sound, produced by atoms vibrating in a material, rather like a Mexican wave travelling through a packed stadium. No single person is the wave, yet the wave moves as if it were an object in its own right.

Still, anyons are now taken seriously as possible building blocks for fault-tolerant quantum computers. An experiment in 2024 showed how swapping them around allows information to be stored in a form that is more resilient to errors. But anyons looked like a special loophole to the rule DHR had established: exotic and useful in the laboratory, but nowhere close to a rebuttal of the standard model’s grand binary.

Code cracked

In three dimensions, making paraparticles work is quite a bit more difficult. In 2021, Toppan tried to introduce a different type of algebra for describing paraparticles in the laboratory. With this maths, he found that ordinary space no longer forced paraparticles to collapse back into fermions or bosons. The trick was to stop looking at particles one by one. On their own, they could still pass for ordinary particles, but in groups their shared quantum state carried an extra mathematical fingerprint, unique to paraparticles.

But serious problems remained. For bosons or fermions, swapping two identical particles in a wave function doesn’t mean this has to happen in the real world. No one is dragging one electron around another. Instead, you are just changing the labels: the particle called A is now B, and vice versa. Since electrons are identical, nothing measurable should change.

Paraparticles are a hypothetical third class of particle, distinct from both matter-forming fermions and force-carrying bosons Stephan Schmitz

Paraparticles seemed different. Swapping their labels also changed something else: their hidden states. Even if it is unclear what that physically corresponds to, the relabelling seemed to have real consequences. Now, put one paraparticle on Earth and another on Pluto, and you’ll find that swapping their labels violates a principle known as “locality”, which says that distant objects can’t influence one another instantaneously.

But a solution to this once-fatal problem has recently come to light. Zhiyuan Wang, then a PhD student at Rice University and now at the Max Planck Institute of Quantum Optics in Germany, was stuck indoors during a covid-19 lockdown and was entertaining himself with maths. “It’s kind of funny,” says Hazzard, his colleague and former supervisor. Wang’s response to lockdown boredom was “playing around with representations of algebra”. But when Wang showed him his calculations, Hazzard was immediately intrigued.

Using a similar but extended version of Toppan’s algebra, Wang and Hazzard tied the mathematics of re-labelling particles back to physical motion. In a paper published in 2025, they showed how a swap could change a paraparticle’s hidden states only if the particles were actually exchanged by moving one around the other. This process would take time, so wouldn’t allow for a faster-than-light signal.

“It’s an absolutely brilliant paper,” says Toppan. Wilczek describes the finding as “interesting” and “intriguing”, but says more theory is needed. Wang and Hazzard have shown how paraparticles behave when exchanged, but not yet the full story of how they move or interact with one another. “Anyons are different in that we can keep track of the whole history of how particles have moved,” he says.

Even so, Wang and Hazzard’s work suggests that paraparticles could exist as quasiparticles that can exist in higher dimensions than anyons. “New types of quasiparticles may give us materials with properties that we just didn’t realise were possible,” says Hazzard. Materials hosting paraparticles could conduct heat or electrical charge in unfamiliar ways, for instance.

Wang also thinks there could be interesting applications in secret communications. Imagine two parties wanting to exchange information without sending a conventional signal that could be intercepted. In principle, swapping paraparticles might allow information to be encoded in their exchange without leaving the same kind of measurable trace.

A new fundamental particle

While the possibility of new quasiparticles could unlock technologies, the bigger prize lies elsewhere: paraparticles not just inside materials, but as a new type of fundamental particle. “The idea that these could be fundamental particles is extremely speculative. It seems a little bit of a desperate idea,” says Hazzard. “But when you talk to people in the community of finding new fundamental particles, it turns out we’re a little desperate.”

No one yet knows quite what role such particles would play, or how they would interact with the particles we already know. But they would almost certainly behave unlike anything in the standard model. Bosons are happy to crowd together, while fermions are barred from doing so. Paraparticles would obey a stranger rule of togetherness: not quite free to pile up, but not quite forbidden from overlapping. They would introduce a new way for particles to share the universe’s quantum real estate.

One especially tempting problem to apply them to would be the mystery of dark matter. We know that galaxies are wrapped in vast halos of unseen matter, but we still don’t know what this is made of. Hazzard has already begun exploring whether paraparticles could fit the bill with cosmologists Andrew Long and Mustafa Amin, also at Rice University.

Paraparticles could obey unfamiliar quantum rules, with hidden states rearranging when identical particles exchange places Stephan Schmitz

They say that the inability of fermions to share the same quantum state imposes a limit. “If the mass of the dark matter particle is below about 1/500,000th of the electron’s mass, then dark matter cannot be made of fermions,” says Long. Very light dark matter candidates – such as the hypothetical particles known as axions, which interact very weekly with ordinary matter and light – are usually treated as bosons for that reason.

But Amin realised that the new social rules of paraparticles could allow them to be light enough to form dark matter halos. The idea is still preliminary, but it is an intriguing proposition. “I think people who work on dark matter would be very receptive to the idea that there’s a whole new knob to turn,” says Long.

Proving paraparticles

Demonstrating that paraparticles exist won’t be easy. Their defining hidden states can’t be measured directly. Instead, physicists must look for them indirectly.

Toppan has recently proposed one such test. Imagine two scientists monitoring a pair of particles before and after they are exchanged. For ordinary bosons or fermions, the measurements should match perfectly. For paraparticles, the hidden states would subtly alter the underlying wave function, creating a telltale asymmetry in the data even though the particles themselves still appear identical.

Hazzard and Bryce Gadway at Penn State University hope to look for this effect in paraparticle-like states in chains of ultracold atoms. It wouldn’t amount to the discovery of a fundamental paraparticle, but it would show that their strange behaviours can emerge in a real physical system.

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Lost in Space-Time Sign up to newsletter

Finding a fundamental paraparticle, on the other hand, would be an even taller order. One possible route to this is through particle accelerators. “What you’d do is accelerate some particles, create a cascade reaction and then observe the outcomes,” says Vedral. That means smashing particles together, letting the collision spray out new particles and then looking for patterns in the debris. In principle, paraparticles could carry the imprint of hidden states changing as particles swap places.

But there might be a way to see evidence in data we already have. If paraparticles contribute to dark matter, dark energy or other cosmic phenomena, their fingerprints might already be hiding in observations of the universe. Vedral and others have suggested looking for their influence in the cosmic microwave background, the relic radiation left over from the big bang. “One of the possibilities is that there is a contribution from some kind of paraparticle interactions,” he says. Kasia Rejzner at the University of York, UK, says this is “the simplest, cheapest way to look for them”.

For now, paraparticles remain a mathematical possibility rather than a physical reality. Much is still unknown, including their mass, spin and full dynamics. Wang and Hazzard are now working to place them within a fully relativistic quantum field theory, the language of modern particle physics. Whether that effort ultimately reveals a new kind of particle or closes the door once again remains to be seen.

But if paraparticles survive such efforts, they may change the standard model in ways we can barely imagine. There is even the possibility, says Hazzard, that some particles we already know as fermions or bosons might reveal paraparticle-like behaviour if measured precisely enough. “Some people have reached out to us saying: ‘How well do we know the electron actually is a fermion?’” he says.

It is a revealing question. Physics can sometimes see reality in black and white. But paraparticles are a vital reminder that nature’s cleanest divisions are often places to look again.
articles.reasoning: The article discusses a significant theoretical breakthrough in fundamental physics — the potential existence of paraparticles, a new class of particles beyond the standard model. This discovery could profoundly impact our understanding of the universe, including mysteries like dark matter, and open new avenues for advanced materials and quantum technologies. The story is focused, detailed, and presents a hopeful scientific advancement with broad implications for physics and technology.
articles.personality_title: New type of particle could change our understanding of the universe
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Physicists have long divided particles into two groups: fermions, which make up matter like electrons and quarks, and bosons, which carry forces like light and gravity. This division forms the basis of the standard model, the main theory explaining the universe’s fundamental building blocks. However, this model cannot explain everything, such as dark matter or gravity.

**What happened** – Recent mathematical research suggests there might be a third type of particle called paraparticles. These particles follow different quantum rules than fermions and bosons. Scientists have developed new math showing how paraparticles could exist in three dimensions without breaking known physical laws. This work was done by researchers including Zhiyuan Wang and Kaden Hazzard, who found ways paraparticles could behave without contradicting principles like the speed of light limit.

**Impact** – If paraparticles exist, they could fill gaps in current physics. For example, they might help explain dark matter, the invisible matter that holds galaxies together. Paraparticles could also lead to new materials with special properties or improve quantum computers. This discovery challenges the long-held idea that only two types of fundamental particles exist and suggests the universe might be more complex than we thought.

**What's next step** – Scientists are now looking for signs of paraparticles in experiments. They hope to detect them indirectly by studying particles in ultracold atoms or high-energy collisions in particle accelerators. Researchers are also checking if paraparticles could have left traces in cosmic radiation from the early universe. Further work will try to fit paraparticles into the full framework of physics to understand how they move and interact.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New math shows a possible third kind of particle, paraparticles, could exist and help explain mysteries like dark matter, opening fresh paths for physics research.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau type de particule pourrait changer notre compréhension de l’univers
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les physiciens divisent depuis longtemps les particules en deux groupes : les fermions, qui composent la matière comme les électrons et les quarks, et les bosons, qui transmettent les forces comme la lumière et la gravité. Cette division est la base du modèle standard, la théorie principale qui explique les éléments fondamentaux de l’univers. Pourtant, ce modèle ne peut pas tout expliquer, notamment la matière noire ou la gravité.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des recherches mathématiques récentes suggèrent qu’il pourrait exister un troisième type de particule appelé paraparticules. Ces particules suivent des règles quantiques différentes de celles des fermions et des bosons. Des scientifiques, dont Zhiyuan Wang et Kaden Hazzard, ont développé de nouveaux calculs montrant que les paraparticules pourraient exister en trois dimensions sans violer les lois physiques connues.

**Impact** – Si les paraparticules existent, elles pourraient combler des lacunes de la physique actuelle. Par exemple, elles pourraient aider à expliquer la matière noire, cette matière invisible qui maintient les galaxies ensemble. Elles pourraient aussi conduire à de nouveaux matériaux aux propriétés spéciales ou améliorer les ordinateurs quantiques. Cette découverte remet en cause l’idée longtemps admise qu’il n’y a que deux types de particules fondamentales et suggère que l’univers est peut-être plus complexe que ce que l’on croyait.

**Prochaines étapes** – Les scientifiques cherchent maintenant des signes de paraparticules dans des expériences. Ils espèrent les détecter indirectement en étudiant des particules dans des atomes ultrafroids ou lors de collisions à haute énergie dans des accélérateurs de particules. Ils vérifient aussi si les paraparticules pourraient avoir laissé des traces dans le rayonnement cosmique issu du début de l’univers. Des travaux supplémentaires tenteront d’intégrer les paraparticules dans le cadre complet de la physique pour comprendre leur mouvement et leurs interactions.

**Phrase clé** – De nouveaux calculs montrent qu’un troisième type de particule, les paraparticules, pourrait exister et aider à expliquer des mystères comme la matière noire, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour la recherche en physique.
articles.personality_title_es: Un nuevo tipo de partícula podría cambiar nuestra comprensión del universo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los físicos han dividido durante mucho tiempo las partículas en dos grupos: fermiones, que forman la materia como electrones y quarks, y bosones, que llevan las fuerzas como la luz y la gravedad. Esta división es la base del modelo estándar, la teoría principal que explica los elementos fundamentales del universo. Sin embargo, este modelo no puede explicar todo, como la materia oscura o la gravedad.

**Qué pasó** – Investigaciones matemáticas recientes sugieren que podría existir un tercer tipo de partícula llamada parapartículas. Estas partículas siguen reglas cuánticas diferentes a las de los fermiones y bosones. Científicos como Zhiyuan Wang y Kaden Hazzard desarrollaron nuevas matemáticas que muestran cómo las parapartículas podrían existir en tres dimensiones sin romper las leyes físicas conocidas.

**Impacto** – Si las parapartículas existen, podrían llenar vacíos en la física actual. Por ejemplo, podrían ayudar a explicar la materia oscura, la materia invisible que mantiene unidas a las galaxias. También podrían conducir a nuevos materiales con propiedades especiales o mejorar las computadoras cuánticas. Este descubrimiento desafía la idea de que solo existen dos tipos de partículas fundamentales y sugiere que el universo podría ser más complejo de lo que pensábamos.

**Próximos pasos** – Los científicos ahora buscan señales de parapartículas en experimentos. Esperan detectarlas indirectamente estudiando partículas en átomos ultrafríos o en colisiones de alta energía en aceleradores de partículas. También están verificando si las parapartículas podrían haber dejado huellas en la radiación cósmica del universo temprano. Más trabajo intentará incluir a las parapartículas en el marco completo de la física para entender cómo se mueven e interactúan.

**Frase clave** – Nuevas matemáticas muestran que podría existir un tercer tipo de partícula, las parapartículas, que ayudarían a explicar misterios como la materia oscura, abriendo nuevos caminos para la investigación en física.
articles.image_prompt: An intricate, softly glowing abstract painting of three intertwined quantum waveforms—two distinct, smoothly flowing shapes representing fermions and bosons gently encircling a mysterious third form subtly shifting and blending between them—set against a warm, muted background of earthy tones, symbolizing the hidden, enigmatic paraparticles bridging the known realms of matter and force.
